exemplar.title:
To what extent do Rizal and Conrad portray the effectivity of using knowledge to
impose colonial power in Noli me Tangere and Heart of Darkness?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent do Rizal and Conrad portray the effectivity of using knowledge to
impose colonial power in Noli me Tangere and Heart of Darkness?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: The title and opening paragraphs establish a precise comparative focus maintained throughout the essay.
The research question is clearly stated in interrogative form and consistently linked to discussion.
Methodology employs close literary analysis and key theoretical sources with a clear outline of analytical phases.
weaknesses: Occasional digressions into general colonial background slightly dilute the tight focus.
Minor wording issues (e.g. âeffectivityâ) affect clarity at points.
No explicit criteria are provided for the selection of theoretical sources.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates topic and purpose accurately, with a wellâfocused research question and clear structure. Methodology is generally complete and appropriate, though the range of sources is not fully comprehensive and selection criteria are implicit. Overall performance fits solidly at the top of the 3â4 band for methodology and the midpoint of the 5â6 band for focus and question, meriting 5/6.

strengths: Confident and coherent knowledge of both primary texts, with accurate contextualized quotations.
Effective integration of major postcolonial theorists (Foucault, Fanon, Bhabha, Said) with narrative analysis.
Consistent and precise use of subject-specific terminology and concepts.
weaknesses: A few typographical errors in names and terms (e.g. âFoucualt,â âeffectivityâ).
Some secondary sources are cited somewhat mechanically without fuller conceptual development.
Minor conceptual slip in classifying Kurtzâs positionality within colonial frameworks.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates excellent understanding of both literary texts and theoretical frameworks, and applies terminology with accuracy and consistency. While there are minor linguistic and conceptual slips, these do not undermine the overall coherence and depth of knowledge. This performance meets the descriptors for the top band, warranting 6/6.

strengths: Research draws on a strong selection of primary and secondary sources directly pertinent to the question.
Analysis is sustained and comparative, with most conclusions well supported by textual and theoretical evidence.
Argument is logically structured, culminating in a coherent synthesis addressing the research question.
weaknesses: Engagement with counter-arguments and alternative viewpoints is limited, reducing evaluative depth.
Some paragraphs verge on summary rather than deep analysis of textual significance.
Critical evaluation could be strengthened by broader reflection on implications of findings.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation exhibits excellent research relevance and depth of analysis, consistently linking evidence to the research question. Evaluation and discussion are good but not fully probing, lacking systematic counterargument engagement. Overall this aligns with the high end of the 10â12 band, justifying a score of 10/12.

strengths: Structure is clearly appropriate with all required sections (title page, abstract/intro, labeled body sections, conclusion, references).
Table of contents and section transitions effectively guide the reader.
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation formatting and missing page numbers in inâtext citations.
Conclusion ends midâsentence and some layout elements (dot leaders, spacing) need refinement.
Page numbers are not visible in the table of contents or main text excerpt.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets most structural requirements, with clear organization and navigation aids. However, inconsistencies in citation style, missing pagination, and minor layout issues prevent a perfect score. The work fits within the 1â2 and 3â4 bands, meriting 3/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The methodology is mostly complete: the student employs close literary analysis, secondary theoretical sources (Foucault, Fanon, Bhabha, Said) and provides a reference list.  Source selection is **generally relevant and informed**, yet the range is not especially wide for an EE (few recent journal articles, limited historical contextual material) and there is no explanation of criteria for selecting critics.  This aligns with a solid but not fully comprehensive approach, placing it near the top of the 3â4 band.

Formatting conventions (title, word count, reference list, section headings, table of contents) are all present and generally accurate.  Minor inconsistencies in in-text citation style and the absence of visible page numbers in the excerpt keep it from perfect execution, so a score of 3 within the 3â4 band is appropriate.

Research evidence is drawn from primary texts and a range of recognised theorists, and it is **mostly pertinent to the investigation**.  While additional, more diverse scholarly voices could strengthen breadth, the material chosen remains clearly relevant and well-connected to the question, meriting entry into the 7â9 âgoodâ band at its lower end.

The studentâs title and opening paragraphs state a precise comparative focus on how knowledge is employed as a âtool of powerâ in two clearly-identified primary texts.  The scope (Rizal and Conrad; knowledge and colonial power) is maintained throughout the outline, section headings and conclusion, showing **consistent purpose and focus**.  Minor wording issues (âeffectivityâ) do not impede clarity, but occasional digressions into general colonial background mean the communication is very strong rather than flawless, warranting the lower end of the top band.

A clear argument is maintained, and the conclusion synthesises findings; however, evaluation of competing viewpoints is limited and counter-arguments are rarely weighed.  Discussion therefore shows **partially critical evaluation**âstronger than adequate but not yet consistently probingâplacing it at the top of the 4â6 band with a score of 6.

The student offers sustained comparative analysis linking textual moments to theoretical concepts and draws supported conclusions about knowledge and power.  Some paragraphs slide into summary or reiterate theoretical points without fresh insight, preventing a higher mark, but **most assertions are evidenced and logically developed**, fitting a score of 7 in the âgoodâ band.

The essay follows the IB required structure (title page with word count, abstract/intro, clearly labelled sections, conclusion, references).  Transitions and sectioning aid reader navigation, so the structure is **clearly appropriate and complete**, justifying the maximum score in this band.

The essay displays **confident knowledge** of both primary textsâquotations are accurate and contextualisedâand of major post-colonial theorists.  The discussion integrates theory with narrative events effectively, demonstrating coherent understanding.  Some secondary sources are cited mechanically, but overall use is effective, supporting a score of 5 within the top band.

Key literary and theoretical termsââinternalisation,â âmimicry,â âOrientalism,â âcolonised intellectualââare **used accurately and consistently**.  Minor errors (spelling âFoucualtâ, âeffectivityâ) are infrequent and do not undermine overall precision, justifying a score of 5.

The research question is explicitly stated in interrogative form and is tightly linked to the central comparative discussion that follows.  It limits the investigation to **âthe extentâ of knowledgeâs effectiveness** within two specified novels, demonstrating an appropriate, focused scope.  Connections to the discussion remain clear across all body sections; however, the question occasionally shifts wording (âefficiency/efficacyâ), so a midpoint (5) in the highest band is most fitting.

annotations.comment: The title succinctly conveys the comparative focus and clear purpose of the investigation, aligning well with the stated research question.
The term âeffectivityâ is nonstandard; revise to âeffectivenessâ for precision and to maintain academic register.
The word count is clearly stated and within the IB limit, meeting formatting requirements.
The introduction adeptly uses Foucault to frame knowledgeâpower relations, demonstrating strong understanding of key concepts.
The overview of colonialism is broad; tighten this section to focus more directly on your comparative research question.
Typographical error: âFoucualtâ should be corrected to âFoucaultâ to maintain accuracy of terminology.
Citations (Memmi, 1974; Foucault, 1977) lack page numbers; include them for IB citation consistency.
The absence of a visible page number on this page should be addressed to comply with IB formatting standards.
The table of contents provides a clear roadmap, meeting structural requirements for navigation.
Page numbers are missing alongside section headings in the table of contents; include them for completeness.
Consider adding dot leaders or consistent spacing for improved readability in the table of contents layout.
The opening of your comparative framing on page 4 is strong, but the text trails off; ensure full sentences to maintain clarity.
The comparative contrast between Ibarra and Kurtz is well articulated, showcasing a clear purpose and focus.
Repetition of the research question here could be replaced with a brief transition into methodology or analytical approach.
Your outline clearly maps the three analytical phases, anchoring the essayâs structure to the research question.
Provide criteria for selecting your theoretical sources to strengthen the transparency of your methodology.
Effective integration of Edward Saidâs concept, but the in-text citation should include year and page.
The paragraph on Foucaultâs bias in knowledge production demonstrates accurate and consistent use of disciplinary vocabulary.
Introduce Andersonâs (1983) concept more fully before applying it to Rizalâs text for smoother conceptual flow.
Conceptual error: Kurtz is a colonizer, not a colonized subject; correct this to maintain analytical accuracy.
The description of epistemic manipulation articulates Foucaultâs framework clearly and accurately.
Clarify Kurtzâs role and status earlier to avoid confusion in comparative analysis.
Including a brief quotation from Foucault would reinforce your theoretical foundation and strengthen your argument.
The framing of âdeliberate blindnessâ using Fanon and Said enhances conceptual depth effectively.
Direct quotes are provided without page references; include page numbers for all citations.
Consider introducing a counterargument to the notion of deliberate blindness to deepen evaluative discussion.
Some paragraphs on page 9 summarize events rather than critically analyze them; focus more on interpreting significance.
The juxtaposition of Ibarraâs refusal and Kurtzâs declaration effectively supports your comparative argument.
Introduce subheadings or paragraph markers within long sections to guide reader through shifts in focus.
The summary on page 10 synthesizes key insights effectively, reinforcing the sustained argument across sections.
Grammatical oversight: âboth the colonized and colonizer individualsâ could be streamlined for clarity.
Explicitly reference the research question phrase âto what extentâ in this summary to maintain tight focus.
Use of Fanonâs âfractureâ metaphor in the breakdown section shows deep engagement with postcolonial theory.
The Kurtz breakdown analysis lacks a direct Conrad quote here; include one to support your interpretation.
Consider merging overlapping analysis of Ibarra and Kurtz breakdowns to avoid repetitive structure.
Application of Bhabhaâs mimicry enriches the argument, demonstrating accurate use of critical terminology.
Ensure Homi Bhabhaâs (1984) reference includes full page and publisher details to adhere to citation standards.
The conclusion effectively synthesizes key comparative insights, directly addressing the research question.
The conclusion truncates mid-sentence (âBoth works ultimately revealâ); complete this thought for closure.
Add a brief reflection on the broader significance of your findings to strengthen the evaluative dimension.
References include key theorists and primary texts, demonstrating broad and relevant source selection.
Typographical error in reference: âFoucualt, M. (1980)â should be âFoucaultâ.
Citation styles vary (publisher details vs. URLs); apply a consistent IB-approved format across all entries.
Italicize all book and article titles consistently to adhere to IB presentation guidelines.
Said (2003) entry lacks publisher location detail; add full publication information for completeness.
